title: Max-Cut and SDP Relaxations: Notes on Approximation Algorithms
excerpt: Working with Francesco Chierichetti on SDP relaxations for Max-Cut gave me a new appreciation for the gap between theory and experiment in combinatorial optimization. These are my working notes from that project.
text:
During my second year at the Orthogonal School, I worked with Francesco Chierichetti on semidefinite programming relaxations and the Max-Cut problem. These are my working notes from that period: they're not a tutorial, but they might be useful to anyone coming at this material for the first time.

You can take a look at my presentation slides for a high-level overview, which I gave at the end of the project in the 4th Orthogonal Weekend, in Procida, Italy.

<!-- images/posts/2026-05-14/group-photo.png -->
![Group photo from the 4th Orthogonal Weekend](https://www.elicsir.it/eventi/quarto-weekend-ortogonale-2025-2026)

The problem

Max-Cut is deceptively simple to state: given a graph $G = (V, E)$, partition the vertices into two sets to maximize the number of edges that cross the partition. It's NP-hard in general, which means we don't expect to solve it exactly in polynomial time. The interesting question is: how well can we approximate it?

The integer formulation assigns a label $xi \in \{-1, +1\}$ to each vertex; an edge $\{i,j\}$ is cut when $xi \neq xj$, i.e. when $xi x_j = -1$:

$$\max{x \in \{-1,+1\}^n} \sum{\{i,j\} \in E} \frac{1 - xi xj}{2}$$

Trivially, a random partition gives you an expected cut of half the edges: a 0.5-approximation. The Goemans-Williamson algorithm from 1995 does substantially better: it achieves an approximation ratio of approximately 0.878. The key insight is to relax the integer problem to a continuous one — each vertex gets a unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$ instead of a scalar in $\{-1,+1\}$ — solve the resulting semidefinite program, and then round the solution back to integers via a random hyperplane.

Why SDP is the right tool here

Semidefinite programs are a generalization of linear programs where the variables are symmetric matrices constrained to be positive semidefinite. They're solvable in polynomial time, and they provide tighter relaxations for many combinatorial problems than LP relaxations do, which is exactly what you
